name: Dinosaurs
description: Paleontology terms about the giants that once ruled the Earth.

group.definition: The geological era from about 252 to 66 million years ago, often called the Age of Dinosaurs. It is divided into three periods: the Triassic, the Jurassic and the Cretaceous.
The first Mesozoic period (252–201 million years ago), when the earliest dinosaurs appeared.
The middle Mesozoic period (201–145 million years ago), dominated by giant sauropods and early theropods.
The final Mesozoic period (145–66 million years ago), ending with the mass extinction of non-avian dinosaurs.
The 'lizard-hipped' order of dinosaurs, grouping the theropods and the long-necked sauropodomorphs.
The 'bird-hipped' order of dinosaurs, made up entirely of herbivores such as Triceratops and Stegosaurus.
A suborder of mostly carnivorous, bipedal saurischians that includes Tyrannosaurus and modern birds.
A suborder of huge, long-necked herbivorous saurischians such as Brachiosaurus and Diplodocus.
A suborder of herbivorous ornithischians, including Iguanodon and the duck-billed hadrosaurs.
A family of large, powerful carnivorous theropods, most famously Tyrannosaurus rex.
A family of extremely long, slender sauropods such as Diplodocus and Apatosaurus.
A family of horned, frilled herbivorous dinosaurs that includes Triceratops.
A family of plated, spike-tailed herbivorous dinosaurs, most famously Stegosaurus.
A family of giant, long-necked sauropods with longer front legs and high shoulders, such as Brachiosaurus.
A family of large, semi-aquatic theropods with crocodile-like snouts and sail-backed bodies, such as Spinosaurus.
A family of heavily armored herbivores with bony plates and a clubbed tail, such as Ankylosaurus.
A family of small, agile feathered theropods with a sickle-shaped claw, including Velociraptor and Deinonychus.
A family of duck-billed herbivores, often with elaborate head crests, such as Parasaurolophus and Edmontosaurus.
The preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms, such as bones, eggs or footprints.
A scientist who studies fossils to understand the history of life on Earth.
The science of naming and classifying organisms into ranked groups such as order, suborder, family, genus and species.
